headlines.title: 4 injured, dozens displaced following fire at Redwood City apartment complex
headlines.description: Dozens of people were displaced and several injured Monday evening when a fire ripped through a Redwood City apartment building. The fire was first reported at 5:18 p.m. at a three-story, 10-unit apartment complex in the 1100 block of Whipple Avenue. The three-alarm fire took an hour to knock down, according to Redwood City Fire Department Deputy Chief Shon Buford. Twenty-three people were displaced and at least four people were transported to the hospital with injuries, he said. Some pets in the building escaped the fire but several were found deceased or are missing. Details as to the cause of the fire are still under investigation at this time, according to Buford.

headlines.title: Miami Dolphins legend and Oakland native Manny Fernandez dies at 79
headlines.description: Former Miami Dolphins defensive lineman and Oakland native Manny Fernandez has died at the age of 79, the team announced on social media. Fernandez was a key member of the Dolphins’ famed “No-Name Defense,” which led Miami to the only undefeated and untied season in NFL history. The Dolphins finished 17-0 during the 1972 season and went on to win the Super Bowl. Fernandez was born in Oakland and attended San Lorenzo High School. He played on all three Dolphins Super Bowl teams in the early 1970s and helped Miami win championships in 1972 and 1973.

headlines.title: Mental health report expected in Molotov cocktail case at Sam Altman's SF home
headlines.description: A mental health report is expected to be released to attorneys Tuesday in the case of a Texas man accused of throwing a Molotov cocktail at the San Francisco home of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman. Defense attorneys allege 20-year-old Daniel Moreno-Gama was experiencing a mental health crisis at the time prosecutors say he threw the Molotov cocktail. The report was one of the initial requests made by the public defender during Moreno-Gama’s arraignment a couple of weeks ago. Prosecutors say Moreno-Gama threw a Molotov cocktail at the gate of Altman’s Russian Hill home during the early morning hours of April 10 with intent to harm. The fire caused damage, but no one was injured. Prosecutors say Moreno-Gama then continued to OpenAI headquarters, where he allegedly attempted to damage property there. State prosecutors have charged Moreno-Gama with nearly a dozen counts, including attempted murder, arson and possession of a destructive device. He is also facing federal charges. Moreno-Gama’s preliminary hearing was originally scheduled for mid-May but has now been pushed back to early August. Attorneys are expected to receive and review the mental health report Monday as the case moves toward that hearing. San Francisco May 15 Artificial intelligence Apr 13 San Francisco Apr 10 Suspect in Molotov cocktail attack on Sam Altman's San Francisco home identified

headlines.title: Los Gatos mother convicted of throwing underage parties faces sentencing
headlines.description: A Los Gatos mother convicted of holding alcohol-fueled sex parties for teens is scheduled to be sentenced Tuesday in Santa Clara County Superior Court. Shannon O’Connor faces more than 30 years in prison after jurors in March convicted her on 48 felony charges. Prosecutors said O’Connor provided alcohol and condoms for parties attended by teenagers as young as 14 and 15 years old and encouraged sex acts during the gatherings. The sentencing hearing is scheduled for 1:30 p.m. Los Gatos Mar 4 Los Gatos mom Mar 4 Investigative reporter examines Los Gatos mom verdict

headlines.title: Video shows Alcatraz ferry hitting San Francisco pier
headlines.description: Dozens of passengers were onboard a cruise Sunday in San Francisco when the boat scraped along the dock and began breaking out windows. The damage was caught on cell phone video. Gia Vang spoke to one of the passengers. Watch her report in the video above.

headlines.title: New questions surround Fairfield police officer's actions seen in viral videos
headlines.description: New videos have surfaced involving an officer seen in previous viral footage taking down a student in Fairfield. The fallout comes after students and parents rallied last week over what they say is excessive use of force. Video that went viral last week shows a police officer hitting a student in the face multiple times while police responded to a fight at Fairfield High School. Another video that recently surfaced shows the same officer pulling an 18-year-old girl out of a vehicle by the hair during a traffic stop last July. “It’s ridiculous that we have an officer with a badge that operates in this kind of way and this kind of manner,” said Berry Accius, a community advocate. Fairfield May 22 Fairfield May 21 The police officer used similar tactics on the high school student last week by pulling the 16-year-old student’s hair. “She shouldn’t be on the force,” said Andrew Holiman, a rapper in Fairfield. Holiman was outraged when he saw what happened to the Fairfield High School teen and he immediately recognized the officer. “Watching the video I noticed it was the officer I had an encounter with myself,” he said. Holiman said he and his friends were shooting a music video in his apartment complex parking lot when the same officer along with nine others came rushing in accusing them of breaking cars. He said the officer approached them very aggressively, making them sit on the curb as police searched their car. When Holiman asked for the officer’s badge number she refused. Police determined Holiman and his friends weren’t breaking the law and ended up releasing the men. But when Holiman saw what happened to the student he knew he had to speak out. “I just felt like being a part of the community is to show that this is a pattern for this officer — she’s violent,” he said. “She’s reckless.” NBC Bay Area reached out to the Fairfield Police Department for comment on the videos that have surfaced since last week’s incident at Fairfield High School. The police chief said the officer involved has been administratively reassigned and an outside investigation will take place. Meanwhile, another protest about the officer’s actions is scheduled Tuesday at Fairfield City Hall.

headlines.title: Considering a summer barbecue? Grilling will cost you more this year
headlines.description: As Memorial Day weekend signals the unofficial start of summer, be prepared to pay more if you’re planning to host a backyard barbecue. Beef prices are up 16% from this time last year, hot dogs are up 11%, seasonings and spices are up 4%, and tomatoes are up 40%. “As we all know, everything’s smaller and everything’s more expensive,” Ricardo Ballin of San Jose said. “The bags of charcoal, you used to be able to struggle carrying them. Now you pick up two bags and I think it was like $15.” NBC Bay Area’s Scott Budman has more in the video report above.

headlines.title: Vigil planned for Bay Area coffee shop owner missing since March
headlines.description: A vigil will be held Monday night for a coffee shop owner who has been missing for two months. Amy Hillyard, 52, co-owner of Farley’s coffee shops, was last seen on March 25 in two Oakland locations: first in the 500 block of Radnor Road in the Cleveland Heights neighborhood about 2 p.m., and then she was spotted on video at Dimond Park at about 4:30 p.m., according to police. A March 31 search of Dimond Park was carried out by OPD along with the Alameda, Contra Costa and Marin county sheriff’s offices, and 60 of Hillyard’s friends, family and volunteers. Hillyard is described as white, 5 feet 4 inches tall, 120 pounds, with blond hair and hazel eyes. Her clothing is said to be light blue jeans, a white shirt, and white tennis shoes with black stripes, according to the California Highway Patrol. She is considered to be at-risk because of medical conditions, police said. On Monday, a “Vigil of Hope & Awareness for Amy Hillyard” will be held from 7 to 8 p.m. at Mandana Plaza Park, 600 Mandana Blvd. in Oakland. The vigil is also being held to mark National Missing Persons Month and the hundreds of missing Bay Area individuals who go missing each year–and their families who are grieving. A reward of $25,000 is being offered for information leading to Hillyard’s safe return, according to the website Bring Amy Home. Anyone with information about her whereabouts is asked to call the Oakland Police Department’s Missing Persons Unit at (510) 238-3641. Oakland Apr 14 Oakland Mar 31 Oakland Mar 27 Missing woman out of Oakland is popular coffee shop owner

headlines.title: Bay Area pays tribute to fallen service members at Memorial Day gatherings
headlines.description: People across the Bay Area gathered Monday to pay their respects to the nation’s fallen service members during various Memorial Day events. At San Francisco National Cemetery in the Presidio, the annual Memorial Day gathering came with added significance. This July, the United States will celebrate 250 years since its founding. In September, the Presidio will celebrate 250 years since being established as a Spanish garrison. The Presidio ceremony included the usual hallmarks that make the event so special to so many. The San Francisco Unified School District JROTC Color Guard presented the flags, the Travis Air Force Base band provided the music and Marine Corps League Detachment 10 presented the rifle salute. Marine Lance Cpl. Juliana Combs was honored by her mother, Lynn Ayala, who read a poem. “For as long as we live, they too will live,” Ayala said. “For they are now a part of us as we remember them.” Memorial Day May 25 Memorial Day May 25 Distinguished guests, including Speaker Emerita Nancy Pelosi and Lt. Gov. Eleni Kounalakis, joined hundreds of veterans in attendance. Veteran Ken Wayne, who also served as the emcee of the event, said there are a number of reflections at this time, particularly as the country finds itself at war once again. “These young men and women who sign up to serve their country, they give an oath to protect our country, to serve our country and put their lives on the line,” he said. “It’s happening again, and it’s sad, but the men and women just do their jobs. They don’t get into the politics. They follow their orders.” For Pelosi, this was her last Memorial Day event at the Presidio as a member of Congress. She vowed to be back again as a private citizen next year. “I feel emotional about it because we’ve been coming here for years and have seen these families — parents who serve, and then they bring their children and then their children’s children,” she said. In the East Bay, moments of remembrance echoed across the region from Mountain View Cemetery in Oakland to the USS Hornet in Alameda. At Mountain View Cemetery, generations of sacrifice were remembered. “It’s important to remember what they did,” said Pam Williams, who comes to the cemetery throughout the year and never misses Memorial Day to honor her friends and neighbors who have lost their lives. Aboard the USS Hornet, retired Navy Capt. Chris Flaherty reflected on the shipmates and friends he lost during nearly three decades of service. “In remembering an individual, an ideal lives on,” Flaherty said. In the South Bay, hundreds of people showed up for Memorial Day ceremonies at Los Gatos Memorial Park. Veterans from as far back as the Civil War are buried at the park.
headlines.source: Ginger Conejero Saab, Sergio Quintana, Velena Jones and Damian Trujillo

headlines.title: Man detained after standoff atop Sonoma County bus
headlines.description: A 47-year-old man suspected of being under the influence of drugs allegedly climbed on top of a Sonoma County bus Sunday night and refused to come down until the next morning, Santa Rosa police said. Officers were called about 9:21 p.m. to the intersection of Mendocino and McConnell avenues, where Pittsburg resident Byron Poindexter had clambered atop the bus, Santa Rosa police said Monday. After police cleared the bus of its passengers, they tried to convince Poindexter to come down, but he refused. Officers didn’t try to physically remove him for fear he or others might be harmed. Shortly before 7 a.m. Monday, Poindexter came down and was safely detained, police said. He was booked into the Sonoma County Main Jail on suspicion of drug paraphernalia and being under the influence of a controlled substance.

headlines.title: 19th Avenue in San Francisco reopens early after Caltrans work
headlines.description: A major road closure along a busy stretch of San Francisco’s 19th Avenue reopened ahead of schedule Monday morning as Caltrans crews wrapped up a weekend paving project. Northbound lanes between Sloat Boulevard and Holloway Avenue fully reopened around 2:30 a.m., about 7 1/2 hours ahead of the scheduled 10 a.m. reopening, according to crews at the scene. Southbound lanes reopened after 6 a.m., with traffic beginning to move smoothly through the area as crews picked up traffic cones and equipment. The closure was part of Caltrans’ latest repaving project along 19th Avenue. During the work, two lanes were closed between Sloat Boulevard and Holloway Avenue, while one lane remained open for transit vehicles, emergency crews and local access. Drone video released by Caltrans showed crews working through the weekend to complete the paving work. Drivers heading northbound from Interstate 280 were advised to use Junipero Serra Boulevard and Sloat Boulevard as alternate routes. Motorists also had the option of using Sunset Boulevard, which runs parallel to 19th Avenue. The closure also prompted temporary service adjustments for several Muni lines, including the 23, 28, 29, 91 and M lines. San Francisco May 22 San Francisco May 11 Caltrans finishes second of three 19th Avenue closures early in San Francisco

headlines.title: San Francisco club spurs debate over access to public tennis courts
headlines.description: Some vocal detractors in San Francisco’s Richmond District have been calling out a private tennis club for occupying public courts. Neighbors said, in the grand scheme of things, not getting a tennis court is far from the biggest problem in the city, but it has prompted a debate about who should be able to access public resources. This all stems from a recent Reddit post, and commenters called out the Rossi Racquet Club for taking up space at the public tennis courts at Rossi Park in the Richmond District. According to the club’s MeetUp activities page, the group takes suggested donations of $5. Critics say they take up highly sought-after space on the public, first-come-first serve courts. The park has three tennis courts, one can be reserved in advance for $7.50, and the other two can be accessed on a first-come, first-served basis. The city also has its own rules for play at public tennis courts. In an interview with the San Francisco Standard, the club’s founder pushed back, saying that people don’t have to pay or be part of the group to join and that his group only plays from 8 a.m. to 12:30 p.m. on weekends. He told the Standard “It’s all drama, we don’t appreciate the internet witch hunt.” But other tennis players NBC Bay Area spoke with disagree. “I think it’s unfair to the local tennis players who are out here trying to have a good time and trying to have easy access to tennis,” said San Francisco resident Calvin Brown, who was playing on the courts Sunday. A few local tennis players also told NBC Bay Area they believe members of this club should have access to the courts just like any other member of the public. NBC Bay Area reached out to the club’s founder but was unable to contact him. NBC Bay Area also reached out to the San Francisco Parks Department for comment
